title: Strings are arrays of characters
instruction:
Every string in Python is a sequence. You can grab any character by its position (index), starting at 0.

Think of a string as a row of mailboxes — each one numbered, each holding one letter.

Run this to see indexing in action.
successMessage: Index 0 = first character. Index -1 = last character. Negative indexing counts from the end — super handy.

title: Slicing — grab a chunk
instruction:
Slicing lets you extract a piece of a string: `text[start:end]`.

The `start` is included, `end` is excluded. Think of it like cutting a cake: you start at one mark and stop *before* the other.

Extract just the word `"Python"` from the string and print it.
hint: "Python" starts at index 7 and ends at index 13. Use greeting[7:13].
starter:
greeting = "Hello, Python!"

# Extract "Python" using slicing
result = greeting[0:5]  # This gets "Hello" — fix it
print(result)
successMessage: greeting[7:13] grabs characters at positions 7, 8, 9, 10, 11, 12 — not 13. The end is always excluded.

title: Useful string methods
instruction:
Python strings have dozens of built-in methods. The most common:

- `.upper()` / `.lower()` — change case
- `.strip()` — remove whitespace from edges
- `.replace(old, new)` — swap text
- `.startswith()` / `.endswith()` — check boundaries

Fix the messy user input below so it prints `"hello world"`.
hint: Chain methods: text.strip().lower() removes whitespace then lowercases.
starter:
text = "   Hello World   "

# Clean this up to print "hello world"
cleaned = text  # Fix this line
print(cleaned)

codeChecks.message: Use .strip() to remove the extra spaces.

successMessage: Method chaining: text.strip().lower() — first trim, then lowercase. You'll use this pattern constantly when cleaning user input.

title: Find and count
instruction:
`.find(text)` returns the index where a substring first appears (-1 if not found).
`.count(text)` counts how many times it appears.

Find the position of `"world"` in the sentence, and count how many `"o"`s there are.
starter:
sentence = "hello world, hello python"

position = 0      # Fix: find "world"
total_os = 0      # Fix: count "o"s

print(f"'world' starts at index {position}")
print(f"There are {total_os} o's")
successMessage: find() gives you the position, count() gives you the total. Both return integers you can use in your logic.

title: Split — break a string apart
instruction:
`.split(separator)` breaks a string into a list of pieces.

Think of it like snapping a Kit-Kat bar — you choose where to break it.

Split the CSV data by commas and print each name on its own line.
hint: Use data.split(",") to get a list, then loop with for name in names: print(name.strip())
starter:
data = "Alice, Bob, Charlie, Diana"

names = data.split(",")
for name in names:
    print(name.strip())
successMessage: CSV parsed in two lines. split() is how you process text data — log files, configs, API responses, user input.

title: Join — stitch a list back together
instruction:
`.join(list)` is the opposite of split — it glues a list of strings into one string.

The string you call `.join()` on becomes the separator. `", ".join(["a","b"])` → `"a, b"`.

Join the words into a sentence separated by spaces.
hint: Use " ".join(words) to combine with spaces between.
starter:
words = ["Python", "is", "awesome"]

sentence = ""  # Fix: join with spaces
print(sentence)
successMessage: split() breaks apart, join() puts back together. They're a natural pair you'll use together all the time.

title: Replace
instruction:
`.replace(old, new)` swaps every occurrence of `old` with `new`.

Replace all spaces with hyphens to create a URL-friendly slug.
starter:
title = "My First Blog Post"

slug = title.lower().replace(" ", "-")
print(slug)
successMessage: lower() + replace() — that's literally how every blog platform generates URL slugs. Real-world Python in two method calls.

title: The `in` operator
instruction:
The simplest way to check if text contains something: the `in` keyword.

`"py" in "python"` → `True`

No method call, no index — just plain English. Check if the email contains `"@"` and print whether it's valid.
successMessage: `in` reads like English: 'if @ is in email'. Python was designed to be readable, and this is a perfect example.

title: Build a text cleaner 🔧
instruction:
Build a function `clean_username(raw)` that:
1. Strips whitespace
2. Converts to lowercase
3. Replaces spaces with underscores
4. Removes any `@` symbols

Test it with the messy inputs below.
starter:
def clean_username(raw):
    # Your code here
    return raw

print(clean_username("  John Doe  "))
print(clean_username("@CoolUser123"))
print(clean_username("  Jane Smith  "))
successMessage: A real utility function. Every web app has something like this in its user registration flow. You just built one.
